---
title: "SwiftUI, Observation, and the AppKit Overlay"
---

SwiftUI builds the settings dashboard. AppKit owns the overlay because the
overlay needs precise window behavior that is awkward to express as a normal
SwiftUI scene.

## A SwiftUI view is a description

A `View` is a lightweight value whose `body` describes desired UI:

```swift
struct DashboardView: View {
    @Bindable var model: ClassroomAppModel

    var body: some View {
        Toggle(
            "Correct finalized captions",
            isOn: $model.correctionEnabled
        )
    }
}
```

SwiftUI may evaluate `body` repeatedly. Do not put model loading, network
requests, or irreversible work directly in it. Event handlers invoke methods
on the long-lived app model.

`$model.correctionEnabled` is a `Binding<Bool>`, essentially a typed getter and
setter pair. It is not a pointer to the property's storage.

## Observation tracks dependencies

`@Observable` instruments property access. While evaluating a view, SwiftUI
records which observable properties were read. A later write invalidates only
dependent views.

The model remains the source of truth for:

- session state and timeline;
- model configuration and diagnostics;
- overlay visibility and geometry;
- sharing status and question queue;
- user defaults mirrored into controls.

This is why services publish events rather than writing views directly.

## Why `NSPanel`

The controller creates:

```swift
panel = NSPanel(
    contentRect: NSRect(x: 0, y: 0, width: 900, height: 180),
    styleMask: [.borderless, .nonactivatingPanel],
    backing: .buffered,
    defer: true
)
```

`.nonactivatingPanel` allows interaction without stealing focus from lecture
software. `.borderless` removes standard chrome. The level, Spaces behavior,
mouse policy, and selected screen are then configured explicitly.

## Hosting SwiftUI inside AppKit

`NSHostingView<CaptionOverlayView>` renders the declarative caption view inside
the imperative AppKit window:

```swift
hostingView.rootView = CaptionOverlayView(
    lines: lines,
    provisional: provisional,
    question: question,
    pendingQuestionCount: pendingQuestionCount,
    fontSize: fontSize
)
```

Replacing `rootView` gives the overlay an immutable snapshot. AppKit owns
window lifetime and geometry; SwiftUI owns text layout inside the content
rectangle.

## The transparent interaction layer

A separate `NSView` sits above the hosting view and interprets mouse gestures.
At mouse-down it records the initial panel frame. Mouse movement uses total
translation from that origin, avoiding lag caused by repeatedly accumulating
rounded deltas.

The controller clamps the result to `screen.visibleFrame`, enforces a minimum
size, and persists one rectangle per display identifier. This matters when the
projector and laptop have different coordinate systems or menu-bar insets.

## Multiple displays

`NSScreen.screens` exposes connected displays. A `CGDirectDisplayID` provides a
stable key during the current display configuration. The overlay is ordered
only on the selected screen, so extended desktop mode can show captions on the
professor's display while the projector remains clean.

Mirror mode is fundamentally different: macOS sends the same framebuffer to
both physical displays. An application cannot put the overlay on only one
mirrored output.

## Scrolling captions

The overlay must prioritize recent text. Its content is placed in a scrolling
container and programmatically anchored to the latest segment. A fixed-height
label with line truncation would eventually show `...` and hide new speech,
which is unacceptable for accessibility.

Final and provisional text remain visually distinct. A queued student question
has a separate marker and count so the professor can notice it without opening
the dashboard.

## Visibility is presentation state

Hiding or clearing the overlay does not erase the timeline:

```text
timeline = authoritative transcript
overlay visibility = whether the window is ordered front
overlay clear point = earliest sequence currently rendered
```

That separation allows voice commands to hide, reveal, or clear presentation
without corrupting the archive or Gemma context.

## Reconstruct the overlay

1. Create a non-activating, borderless `NSPanel`.
2. Embed a SwiftUI caption view in `NSHostingView`.
3. Add a transparent AppKit interaction view above it.
4. Implement move and resize from an initial frame plus total drag translation.
5. Clamp geometry to the selected screen and persist it.
6. Render finalized text, provisional text, and questions as distinct data.
7. Keep visibility and clear position outside the transcript model.
